speaker: interviewee
text: May we start?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about an air attack at Auschwitz Buna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I use the telephone? That would interrupt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talking about an air attack at Auschwitz Buna.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and I was laying in that crater, and I remember I was in a shock-- in shock, and the buddy, next buddy, he saved my life. I didn't even know it. He looked at me and my-- my shirt was soaked in blood from my-- I was bleeding. I would have bled to death.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- I remember he turned me on my belly. He tore up the shirt and put a-- what do you call it? A-- tourniquet. Tourniquet on my neck to stop the bleeding. And that probably saved my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was laying there for several hours. And I was already considered a not heavy, not severely wounded, but many of them lost their legs, their limbs, and everything. Trucks came and picked them up. And my buddies from my kommando, they made a makeshift stretcher, and it brought me back in there because we had to walk almost [? 45 ?] miles, and I wasn't able. And they brought me, took me into the-- back to the camp at Buna. Buna had a dispensary. They called it a dispensary, and they took me in there. And I remember it was already filled up with-- with wounded and half-dead. And I was laid-- that was on a Sunday. I was laying on the floor, because they didn't have no room, till Thursday. I-- in the meantime, I got infection. I remember I-- I couldn't even speak. I couldn't drink. I couldn't take anything. It got infected. Finally on Thursday, the doctor-- it had one doctor, Jewish surgeon. His name was Grossman, I remember right now. But at 8:00, he came and he checked it, and he said I will need operate, to be operated on. They took me in Friday. They operated on me. And I remember even now, when they woke me up after the operation, the doctor showed me the splinter was about that-- he said, you're a fortunate man. He said another split hair would have-- would have severed my main artery. I-- I would have been killed. But my troubles had just started. I remember I was there for two months. I know a lot of people couldn't believe it at Auschwitz, because they don't-- in Auschwitz, if you were sick or some severe, they didn't bother. They took you away to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: What fortunate for me-- you see, the Red Cross used to come in every few months for its check and inspection. And I remember they wanted to show it to the Red Cross. I remember when the Red Cross came from Switzerland, and these SS men-- I was in-- on the bunk laying, and the SS men came up to me with this Red Cross man, and they asked me, how old are you? I said 19.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember he said to the-- to the man, you see, the Americans almost killed him, and we just cured him and-- and brought him back to life. And that was-- everything was for propaganda. Maybe that's the reason they kept me there and they treated me, because the treatment was terrible. I got pains and had no painkillers. It was terrible. The pains were unbearable.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I lost so much weight there because I couldn't eat my-- for my-- I don't know how-- how I survived that thing. I remember that was already October, November '44. One day, I did-- the orderly had to come. You know, the Germans had-- everything had to be [INAUDIBLE]. Here they had to change the-- my-- what do you call it? The paper, you know, what they had to put around my neck. The bandage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bandage. Just couldn't think of it. All they had is toilet paper. They didn't have a real bandage. And even the-- the-- what they put was actually from car grease, and that smelled terrible. And every few days, they had to come and squeeze out the pus. And that was so painful. I used to scream and holler. One day, I just resisted. I said to the orderly, I'm not-- you're not going to do it no more. I don't care what. And he start-- I starts to fight him back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came with a doctor, Dr. Grossman. He was this surgeon. And he looked at me, and he said, you know, this is the procedure. We have to do that. And he was very friendly, and he start talking with me. And he asked me, what is your name? I told him my name is Kolender. He said, Kolender? I had a very good friend. He's also from Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was from Berlin. My neighbor, he was a very good friend. His name was Saul Kolender.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, Saul Kolender? That's my uncle, my father's brother. And we became very good friends. And he looked out for me, and he gave orders not to do it no more. And-- he was-- actually, he saved my life, too, because he had, as a doctor, he had some pills, which he sneaked into me so nobody would see, to kill my pains.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was after a while, I remember he called me in to his room, to his office. He had a little office there. He said, Kolender, I'm going to discharge you now. Now, he says. I said, doctor, I was still weak. I lost-- and I was still skinny. I lost a lot of weight. He said, they're going to liquidate the-- the hospital. You better leave now. He gave me these discharge papers and he sent, with the orderly, he sent me back to my barracks with a letter. He knew the alteste, blockalteste. He was also a German Jew. He knew him, so he told him to please treat me kindly. And I remember the same night, trucks came in and they liquidated the hospital. Took out everybody, and they took them out to the crematoriums, the gas chambers. So that was another break.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said in a dispensary that you had an operation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anesthesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, anesthesia. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that the orderly was German?

speaker: interviewee
text: German Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: German Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: German Jews, yes. And the doctor, Grossman, was also a German Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to Dr. Grossman when they liquidated the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw him late-- later on. He used to come-- He used to come to the barracks, and he marched with us to-- I saw him during that march. I just happened to see him. But he was already a middle-aged man, and I don't think he made it. You know, the march was-- well, we'll get to it a little later. OK. And that was already November, and I went back to the barracks. And then January--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the barrack that you were in-- barrack 11 was destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's still there. I saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When Americans bombed, you were in a building?

speaker: interviewee
text: They bombed Buna, which was several miles, about five, six miles, far away from Buna. They bombed the [? AG ?] farm industry, which was that big military complex.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were on the farm when you got the splinter in your neck?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a building, not in farm, in a-- in-- it was a warehouse. That's where they bombed, and it was a direct hit, and that's when I got hit. And fortunately, my neck-- my back was to the door. Had I been this way, I would have gotten it right in the mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to Barrack 11?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to the barrack, and fortunately, the blockalteste was very kind to me. He didn't give me-- he kept me in the barracks. I was to clean the barracks. He gave me easy job. And I was there till January.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the barracks, did you observe Jewish holidays in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did. We did. Yom Kippur we observed. Of course, we didn't fast, because you couldn't. But we did observe the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pray?

speaker: interviewee
text: We prayed, only we didn't have no books, but we prayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any other violence at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, at least, they didn't shoot at random. We could see people dying in Auschwitz in the barracks from starvation, malnutrition, and all kind, but violence not per se. Like the SS men come and shoot, no. We didn't see that. We saw violence among the inmates, too. We'd been fighting, killing each other for-- people became so inhumane. They would kill for a slightest little thing. You know. For a piece of bread they would kill. I could see-- we could see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother was in a different barrack than you at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was next barrack, and I think it was barrack 12. So we could see each other every day. And we were there-- of course, he came to me, visit me in the hospital all this-- all this time, and that helped me a lot. He encouraged me, and he said, just hold-- just hold-- hold on. And the news were good already in 1944, that Americans went into France and were have-- France was already occupied by the Americans, and things started looking good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get that kind of news?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, we worked with civilians. This military complex was a lot of civilians.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German civilians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Poles. Poles. Poles. And they sneak in paper, magazines. You could read it. I even also worked with English. I forgot to tell you. We worked with English prisoners, POWs. And they were terrific. They-- you see, they used to get Red Cross packages, and they had plenty of food, and we-- we didn't. So I had-- of course, they had to be very careful. If SS men saw it, they were punished. I remember this-- all I remember, his name was Jack. Jack. He was a tall fellow. He was a sergeant. He had-- and every morning, he used-- he told me-- he showed me. He put on a-- on a plate piece of chocolate or a piece of bread, something extra. He left it, and I went in and picked it up. So. And finally, then, they were moved. I remember they moved them. By the end, they moved them away. Well, they were a good source of sustenance. You know, they helped us a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything about the resistance at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: We find-- I found out about it after the war. We didn't know it. We didn't know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that you would leave Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I find out? They came. By January the 18th, we didn't go to work. Everybody stayed in the barrack, and the alteste came. He said you have to get ready. We going to be moved out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945, January the 18th. So we knew that-- as a matter of fact, we could hear from way off in the distance. You could hear heavy artillery. So we knew it's the Russians. They couldn't hide everything from us. They send-- we sensed it. That was in middle-- I think it was a Tuesday. And-- they throw us-- they gave us each one piece of bread, one half of one portion of bread. They said it wouldn't take long, and-- and fortunately for us, we could take out blankets. At Buna, we had blankets. Everybody had one blanket. And I had my utensils. You know, we had this utensil for soup and a cup for coffee. So we-- we could take these with this. The reason I said that-- I will tell you later why this helped us a lot. And we marched the whole night-- that's from Auschwitz to Gliwice, which was almost 70 kilometers. And here the snow was about two feet tall. It was bitter cold, and here we had always wooden shoes or [INAUDIBLE] shoes. It was almost impossible to walk, and we had to run, literally run, because we could hear-- at night, you could see from the heavy artillery, from the muzzles, you could see the-- the bullets, the fired artillery. They could have been maybe 20 or 30 miles away from us. And the SS men, they just said run. The first few hours was fair, but then most people got tired. They couldn't-- couldn't run, so a lot of people just dropped. And anybody who dropped, if you couldn't make it, you could hear the pistol shots every few minutes. It must have been by the hundreds. Literally, the whole road was littered with-- with bodies. And I was walking with my brother all this time. And I remember it was already middle of the night. It was maybe about six or seven hours, and we were all exhausted. But we still had to keep on going, because you knew it's-- if you don't, you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to me, let's escape. I said, escape? You're now in-- you're still in Silesia, which is-- mostly was still German. With this kind of weather, no food, who's going to help you? You-- it's certain death.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he just kept insisting that we are not going to survive, so let's at least give it a try. And I said, no, this is definitely committing suicide. He just wouldn't listen to me. And just in all the commotion, I don't-- didn't see him anymore. He just disappeared. He must have escaped. And I never saw him again. He must-- either he'd been killed, or-- I have no idea. I would say that it was they caught him and they killed him. We got into Gliwice next morning. It was about maybe 10 o'clock. And I could look from back, and we could see a small-- we were thousands of us, and you could see it was much less, because so many of them just didn't make it. I do still remember just before we got to Gliwice. It was on a hill, and I could see down-- maybe it was a few kilometer. We could see the railway station. But my legs literally gave up. I just couldn't-- you couldn't lift it no more.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I said, let me sit down or lay down, and just-- and it must have been an angel or-- I don't know. Then somebody behind me kicked me hard. And he said, shame on you. He said it in Yiddish. You want to get-- You're so close now. You-- you just give up? And he-- he just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I just had found my second wind, and I got up, and we made it. I made it. Of course, we stayed there for about a day in that railroad station. There was thousands of us. There must have been 50,000. Nothing to eat, cold, bitter cold. I couldn't get in the barracks. They were packed. Finally, next day, they came, SS men, and then we start moving to the railroad. And they packed us like sardines, open wagons. Those wagons can take maybe 40, 50 people, and they put about 120 to 150. I remember we were packed like sardines. You couldn't turn. You couldn't move. I was lucky. I was one of the first, so I grabbed a corner like that, and I just leaned on it. And I wouldn't give this up for nothing in the world, because if you gave up-- at least I could lean on something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the first few hours, it was quiet, I remember. Then everybody got restless and got tired. How long can you stay on your feet without moving, without anything? And the commotion started, pandemonium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody start fighting. You know, people came and like animals. And imagine yourself-- you stay on a corner and with so many, and you cannot move. You cannot do anything. You couldn't lay down, and your feet-- your feet are just falling apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people starts fighting each other, and some people start jumping. So literally, they didn't care. And the SS men, right away, anybody who jumped, they just shot them. There were guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and finally, after several hours, the train start moving. It start moving, and the same thing. And every day it got less and less. People start jumping. They just didn't care anymore. They couldn't take it. And it got less and less and less, I remember. And fortunately for us, it was snowing, and we had utensils, so we could scoop up-- I had a piece of string and scooped up, while the train stopped slow, I scooped up the snow and-- and licked the snow. That kept us alive. Took us 10 days. Finally, we reached Nordhausen, which is an earlier concentration camp, and there they unloaded us. You wouldn't believe it. From the 120 or 140, I don't know, there was 20 left of us. 20. Every night, we just-- we just threw out-- you know, throw away. I mean, we picked them up, but it was people just died in front of us. I remember I talked to this fellow. We talked. We sat, and we talked, then all the sudden, he just keeled over. And I looked at him. He's dead. But that's the way it was. When we got to, finally, Nordhausen, we got to Nordhausen. I remember they unloaded us. I could hardly walk. I was-- those inmates had to help us to-- to walk to the camp. We were in the camp in another 10 days there, and then they moved us again. The English came from another direction, so they moved us again. They put us again on trains, and this time they moved us to Thuringen, which is the-- Dora. The name of the camp was Dora. It was in the mountains, Over there, they-- they-- where they used there V-1 and V-2 against England. You know, it was the mountains. And we were there about six weeks. We worked in the inside. We dynamited the inside, the mountains, and we built, you know, storage rooms and so forth. The only good thing about it was it was cold, bitter cold, but we worked in-- in the mountains, in there, so it wasn't-- so it wasn't too cold. We were there till April.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your health like at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Health? I was-- it's amazing. I was still in good health. I was still in good health. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were there till about April. I think it was April.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. About April. Then the orders came again, move again, because from every direction-- the Americans came at it from this side. They said they have to move.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put us again on trains, and they moved us to Czechoslovakia. That was still wasn't occupied. It wasn't still by the-- by neither one.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we stopped near Prague. It was April the 20th. And the reason I remember this well because April the 20th was Hitler's birthday, and the SS came and they gave us extra piece of margarine. That was in honor of the Fuhrer, they said. And it was a Friday morning. It was early in the morning. But this time we were in-- in closed train, a closed cattle train. So-- and we weren't-- they just packed us like-- there were about 80 of us. It was still in-- although it was very cold outside, but inside was very hot, you know, with so many people. So most of us took off our clothes. I had-- all I had is my shorts. About 8 o'clock, just before we started-- give us the-- something to eat, the alarm sounded, and it didn't take long. And all of a sudden, you could see swarms of planes, American fighter planes. And they swooped down and they strafed our train, because they didn't know that-- they thought maybe it's soldiers, German soldiers. And my instinct told me something. I noticed the guards, the SS guards, they jumped the wagon and then they got underneath to cover. And my instinct told me, you better run. And I jumped, me and another fellow. We jumped the train. And then we--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get outside of the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was open. It was open. They just were going to feed us, and somehow the doors were open. And it-- and we jumped. I remember I could see the machine guns were fly-- they were-- there were flying all over bullets, all over me. I don't know how I didn't get hit. And I was, believe or not, naked. All I had is my shorts. And we saw the woods were right-- took to the woods. And we ran. I went-- I said to him, let's run and run away from here. This is the only chance we got. I remember I ran for about 15 minutes without stopping. And it was very dense forest. I got bruised and fell down. Nothing-- it didn't-- it didn't bother me. Later on, I found I was bleeding all over. My whole body was bruised. Anyway, after 15 minutes, we stopped. We took a rest because we noticed we were a few miles away from the-- from the train. Now we had to decide what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: We both were freezing cold. It was bitter cold. What do we do now? So we decided we have to go to a farmer, something, maybe at first to get something to eat. And maybe they'll give us some clothes, something, because it was bitter cold. So we start walking. We walk. From a distance, we could see a little house with-- a farmhouse, you know, those old houses. So we decided-- we knew it was Czechoslovakia, and we weren't afraid because we knew the Czechs were very-- they were-- they hated the Germans too, because they were occupied by the Germans. So we decided to go in there. We knocked on the door. I'll never forget. It was this old man and a lady, this little old lady and her son.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they saw us, they cross-- they [? crucified ?] themselves. We looked like people from Mars, naked and here's bitter cold outside. And fortunately, we could communicate, because Czech and-- and Polish is very similar. So we communicate. We told them who we are, and we just escaped and we need help. Right away, I remember, she gave us water-- hot water to wash ourselves. We were bleeding all over. You know. Cleaned ourselves up, and she-- they gave us some old clothes what he had, old torn socks, torn shoes. But we didn't care. And I mean, he gave us food. Now listen to that. That was the biggest mistake. We almost died right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they gave us the bread, it's fine. He had just the fresh bread taken out from the oven. You know how they bake themselves? But she gave us milk which wasn't pasteurized and sauerkraut. This I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was poison, especially here. We were so skinny, the stomach couldn't-- couldn't take it. And we ate like there is no tomorrow. I remember that was so good. I must have eaten about two pounds of bread, or maybe more than that, and the kraut and the milk. Everything was fine. And they gave us-- of course, we go in the stable, lay down and rest. After a couple hours, I get sick. I got dysentery, start bleeding from the front, from the rear, and I got a fever. I-- I would've died right then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my friend ran back to the man, and he said I'm very sick. Can help? He needs some help. That man went back to town. He went to Prague with his horse and buggy. I could see that.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a while, we were scared. I said, well, I hope he's not going for the Germans. You know, you-- you were scared. But he came with a doctor from Prague. That was a-- he took a tremendous chance. And I remember the doctor gave me a shot, and he gave me paregoric. It was very bitter liquid, and it settled my stomach and I survived. If not this, I probably would've died right there. And the fever were gone next morning. Then next morning the farmer came. He said, you know, there's a lot of Germans. They start looking for you. The Gestapo found out-- you know, from Prague, they found out that a lot inmates escaped, and they were looking in the woods. He said, it's very dangerous. If they catch us here, we'll all be killed. So he told us, I'll give you a shovel. And at night, he took us-- we followed him. He give us signals. And we went deep in the forest right there and we dug a foxhole, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] deep foxhole. We covered it up with-- with leaves. And we lived there till May the 7th, in the foxhole, because the farmer brought us-- every evening, he brought us soup and bread. And that's the way we survived. May the 7th, I was liberated.
